questions.question: What effect can the stigma attached to mental health have on a person?
options: It removes the need for any professional support
It can stop them asking for help and leave them feeling ashamed or isolated
It speeds up their recovery from a mental health condition
It has no real effect on how a person feels

questions.question: According to the material, which of these best describes a delusion?
options: A lasting low mood with loss of interest and tiredness
A strongly held belief that is not based in fact
Sensing things that other people do not sense
Marked swings between very low and high-energy periods

questions.question: Which type of dementia often affects younger people and can change personality, behaviour or language more than memory at first?
options: Lewy body dementia
Alzheimer's disease
Vascular dementia
Frontotemporal dementia

questions.question: The course teaches that behind almost every distress reaction there is:
options: a deliberate attempt to be difficult
an unmet need the person is trying to express
a symptom that should simply be managed and stopped
a normal part of ageing that can be ignored

questions.question: A man living with dementia keeps trying to go outside. Knowing that he was a lifelong gardener most helps you to:
options: understand the meaning behind his wish and respond in a person-centred way
correct him firmly about the facts of his situation
work out which type of dementia he has
insist he stays indoors for his own good

questions.question: Being formally detained under the Mental Health Act 1983 is often referred to as being:
options: sectioned
discharged
safeguarded
assessed

questions.question: Which statement about mental capacity is correct according to the material?
options: A person living with dementia never has capacity for any decision
Capacity is decided once and then never changes
Care workers are responsible for formally assessing a person's capacity
Capacity is specific to each decision and each moment

questions.question: Good wellbeing grows from everyday things like connection, purpose, activity and control. Which is an example of supporting a person's sense of control?
options: Giving them chances to fold laundry or tend a plant
Encouraging a walk in the garden for fresh air
Helping them stay in touch with family and friends
Offering real choices such as what to wear or when to get up

questions.question: A person you support talks about harming themselves. What does the material say you should do?
options: Treat it seriously and escalate straight away
Wait to see if the feeling passes on its own
Keep it to yourself unless it happens again
Tell them not to be so dramatic

questions.question: Delirium is not the same as dementia, although the two can occur together.
options: True
False

questions.question: A diagnosis of dementia describes who a person is.
options: True
False

questions.question: When looking for the unmet need behind a distress reaction, which of these are possible causes the material suggests you consider? (Select all that apply)
options: Physical needs such as pain, hunger or needing the toilet
The person deliberately trying to annoy staff
Emotional needs such as fear, boredom or loneliness
Environmental factors such as noise or unfamiliar surroundings

questions.question: Which of the following are warning signs of delirium described in the material? (Select all that apply)
options: Symptoms that fluctuate, often worse later in the day
Gradual memory loss developing slowly over many years
Changes in alertness, such as being drowsy or agitated
New or worsening confusion that comes on quickly

questions.question: Instead of the unhelpful term 'challenging behaviour', the course prefers to speak of distress ___, which are understandable responses from someone who is upset or frightened.
Where a person needs to be deprived of their liberty to keep them safe, the Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards, abbreviated ___, provide legal protection and oversight.
